books.title: Leadership and Self-Deception
books.author: The Arbinger Institute
books.context: Ethan recommends this book — taught him to see others not as obstacles to what you want but as human beings trying to do their best, which changes how you negotiate.

books.title: Extreme Ownership
books.author: Jocko Willink
books.context: Referenced when urging engineers to take control of their careers instead of playing victim to a bad manager.

books.title: What Got You Here Won't Get You There
books.author: Marshall Goldsmith
books.context: Classic book on leveling up: the skills that got you to one level won't take you to the next — you must let go of hard skills and learn to delegate and communicate.

books.title: So Good They Can't Ignore You
books.author: Cal Newport
books.context: Ethan notes the irony — Newport's thesis is about technical excellence, but Newport himself has the social skills to teach at Princeton and write bestsellers. Technical skill alone is never enough.

people.name: Ethan Evans
people.role: Former Amazon VP; executive coach and newsletter author
people.context: Guest of the episode — 15 years at Amazon, retired VP, now coaches engineers and managers on career navigation and corporate politics.

people.name: Ryan Peterman
people.role: Software engineer and host of The Developing Dev podcast/newsletter
people.context: Interviewer — former Meta engineer who asks Ethan to go deep on transparency about reorgs, firings, and promotions.

people.name: Jeff Bezos
people.role: Founder and former CEO of Amazon
people.context: Cited in story about an entry-level engineer who emailed Bezos to get his Cloud Drive proposal heard at an invite-only engineering poster session.

people.name: Andy Jassy
people.role: CEO of Amazon (former CEO of AWS)
people.context: Ethan cites Andy as one of his hardest managers — quoted on absolving AWS principal engineers from mentoring duties and accepting that some specialists have unique leverage.

people.name: Mike Tyson
people.context: Quoted — 'Everyone has a plan until they get punched in the face' — applied to how org plans survive contact with reality.

people.name: Jocko Willink
people.role: Former Navy SEAL officer; author and leadership speaker
people.context: Cited as author of Extreme Ownership when Ethan urges engineers to take agency over their careers.

people.name: Cal Newport
people.role: Computer science professor at Georgetown and author
people.context: Author of So Good They Can't Ignore You — cited as a model of combining technical depth with the social skills to communicate it. Ethan notes Newport teaches at Princeton (actually Georgetown).

people.name: Marshall Goldsmith
people.context: Author of the classic What Got You Here Won't Get You There on the skill shifts required at each career level.

concepts.name: Empire building
concepts.context: Leaders pursuing raw headcount over impact because headcount is easier to measure than impact — rewarded by written thresholds like needing 80–90 reports to make director at Amazon.

concepts.name: Constructive termination (constructive dismissal)
concepts.context: Legal concept Ethan navigates around when having a 'maybe this isn't working out' talk — if you mention firing, any future action becomes evidence you'd already decided, creating legal risk.

concepts.name: Window seat (madogiwazoku)
concepts.context: Japanese corporate concept — being given a window seat signals exile to irrelevance. Ethan uses it to explain how leaders are managed out by being given 'other' / low-status scope.

concepts.name: Plausible deniability
concepts.context: Political term Ethan applies to slippery coworkers who keep unethical behavior ambiguous enough to deny.

concepts.name: Performance improvement plan (PIP)
concepts.context: Ethan's blunt coaching — by the time a manager files a formal PIP, they've already exhausted other options and just want you gone; survival is very unlikely.

concepts.name: Executive presence
concepts.context: Ethan teaches this as a skill — notes that Darth Vader and Palpatine had it in spades, so the skill itself is morally neutral and motive is what matters.

concepts.name: Magnificent Seven (stocks)
concepts.context: Referenced when noting that the largest US tech companies are now mostly flat in headcount, fueling cannibalistic scope grabs instead of new hiring.

concepts.name: Emotional intelligence (EQ)
concepts.context: Ethan paraphrases the common saying: IQ without EQ is a waste of IQ — engineering career progress depends on interpersonal skill as much as technical skill.

related_posts.reason: Both are deep-dive career advice pieces from senior operators — this episode complements Andrew Ng and Lawrence Moroney's technical career guidance with the corporate-politics layer that actually controls promotion and scope.

The roadmap focuses on technical skills to rise as an engineer; Ethan's transparency on reorgs, allies, and promo packets covers the non-technical skills that decide whether that roadmap actually lands you a promotion.

Shiller's behavioral finance and Ethan's 'polite fiction' / narrative-over-facts insight share the same underlying thesis — humans make emotional decisions and then rationalize them, which shapes both markets and corporate politics.
